growth_records.strain: AWRIB429 (Lalvin VP-41 commercial MLF starter isolate)

up.units: g/L monitored (single carbon source, 5 g/L feed)

growth_records.cite: Onetto et al. Analysis of Transcriptomic Response to SO2 by Oenococcus oeni Growing in Continuous Culture. Microbiology Spectrum (2021). DOI:10.1128/Spectrum.01154-21. [PMC8510247]
growth_records.snippet: CCOo medium was supplied ... average flowrate of 7.6 +/- 0.3 ml/h; dilution rate of 0.030 +/- 0.001 h-1 ... Cell viability, fructose consumption, and lactic and acetic acid production were monitored throughout the experiment
growth_records.notes: Steady-state anaerobic chemostat on fructose sole carbon at wine-like pH 3.5, 22C. D=0.030 h-1 (measured from 7.6 mL/h feed); at steady state mu=D=0.030 /h. td=ln2/mu=0.693/0.030=23.1 h (calculated). Full CCOo medium composition stated. Heterofermentative O. oeni consumes fructose, produces lactic+acetic acid (+CO2). Baseline steady-state absolute fluxes/concentrations not tabulated numerically in main text; qualitative consumption/production monitored by HPLC. Under high-SO2 perturbation (10 mg/L) fructose rose to 4.1 g/L and lactic/acetic fell to 0.1/0.2 g/L at 96 h (viability 6.5->7.2 log CFU/mL), i.e. metabolism inhibited - transient stress, not a steady growth condition.

growth_records.medium: MaxOeno (chemically defined wine-like medium), 0% v/v ethanol

up.method: HPLC (Aminex HPX-87H), specific consumption rate vs biomass; values in Figure 3B (not tabulated numerically)
HPLC; specific consumption ~2x faster than glucose in phase I (Figure 3A); numeric values figure-only
HPLC; >90% consumed in phase I, ~30% faster than L-lactate production (Figure 4A); numeric values figure-only
HPLC; >90% consumed in phase I (Figure 4B); numeric values figure-only
UHPLC/MS (Ellman derivatization); fastest specific consumption among amino acids, consumed in phases I and II (Figure 5A); numeric values figure-only

sec.method: HPLC; specific production rate, phase I, 0% ethanol
HPLC; specific production rate, phase I, 0% ethanol
UHPLC/MS (DATAN derivatization); phase I range across all ethanol conditions
UHPLC/MS; phase I range across all ethanol conditions, >10x slower than L-lactate
HPLC; maximum cumulative acetate reached in cultures without ethanol (2nd most abundant product)

mfa.note: FBA-predicted (iSM454 model constrained by experimental rates), phase I, 0% ethanol; increases to 4.8 at 12% ethanol
FBA-predicted, phase I, 0% ethanol; decreases to 1.3 at 12% ethanol

growth_records.cite: Contreras, Angela et al. Mapping the Physiological Response of Oenococcus oeni to Ethanol Stress Using an Extended Genome-Scale Metabolic Model. Frontiers in Microbiology (2018). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2018.00291. [PMC5838312]
growth_records.method: reported (max specific growth rate, Table 1); doubling time calculated td=ln2/mu=0.6931/0.021=33.0 h
growth_records.snippet: In the absence of ethanol, specific production rates for mannitol and erythritol were 1.25 mmol gDCW-1 h-1 and 0.95 mmol gDCW-1 h-1, respectively. ... 0 ethanol Maximum specific growth rate 0.021 h-1, Maximum biomass 0.854 gDCW L-1 (Table 1
growth_records.notes: Batch cultures of O. oeni PSU-1 in defined MaxOeno medium at 25C, pH 4.8, static airtight flasks. mu=0.021/h is the maximum (phase I) specific growth rate from Table 1 (triplicate, CV<20%). Doubling time td=ln2/mu=0.6931/0.021=33.0 h. Substrate uptake (glucose, fructose, malate, citrate, amino acids) and product (mannitol, erythritol, L-/D-lactate, acetate) specific rates are experimental (HPLC/UHPLC-MS) but reported graphically (Figures 3-5); only 0%-ethanol phase-I polyol rates (mannitol 1.25, erythritol 0.95) and the phase-I lactate ranges (L-lactate 1.5-3.0, D-lactate 0.15-0.20 mmol/gDCW/h) are given numerically in text. Acetate 50 mmol/gDCW is a cumulative maximum (no time basis). F0F1-ATPase (2.9) and phosphoketolase (2.1) ATP fluxes are FBA model predictions from iSM454, not measured.

growth_records.medium: MaxOeno (chemically defined wine-like medium), 3% v/v ethanol

up.method: HPLC; consumed faster at higher ethanol (Figure 4A); numeric figure-only
UHPLC/MS; specific consumption increases with ethanol (Figure 5A); numeric figure-only

sec.method: UHPLC/MS; phase I range across ethanol conditions
UHPLC/MS; phase I range across ethanol conditions

growth_records.cite: Contreras, Angela et al. Mapping the Physiological Response of Oenococcus oeni to Ethanol Stress Using an Extended Genome-Scale Metabolic Model. Frontiers in Microbiology (2018). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2018.00291. [PMC5838312]
growth_records.snippet: 3 (ethanol % v/v) Maximum biomass 0.76 gDCW L-1, Maximum specific growth rate 0.018 h-1 (Table 1).
growth_records.notes: mu=0.018/h max specific growth rate at 3% ethanol (Table 1); td=ln2/0.018=38.5 h. Max biomass 0.76 gDCW/L. Substrate/product specific rates experimental but reported graphically; lactate phase-I ranges (L 1.5-3.0, D 0.15-0.20 mmol/gDCW/h) span all ethanol conditions. Polyol and amino-acid specific rates rise with ethanol but are figure-only (no numeric text values for 3%).

growth_records.notes: mu=0.016/h at 6% ethanol (Table 1); td=ln2/0.016=43.3 h. Max biomass 0.585 gDCW/L. Detailed substrate/product specific rates are experimental but graphical only; lactate phase-I ranges span all ethanol conditions.

growth_records.cite: Contreras, Angela et al. Mapping the Physiological Response of Oenococcus oeni to Ethanol Stress Using an Extended Genome-Scale Metabolic Model. Frontiers in Microbiology (2018). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2018.00291. [PMC5838312]
growth_records.snippet: 9 (ethanol % v/v) Maximum biomass 0.452 gDCW L-1, Maximum specific growth rate 0.014 h-1 (Table 1). Cultures containing 9% ethanol required 10 times more NGAM during phase I than cultures without ethanol.
growth_records.notes: mu=0.014/h at 9% ethanol (Table 1); td=ln2/0.014=49.5 h. Max biomass 0.452 gDCW/L. Model estimated ~10x higher NGAM (non-growth associated maintenance ATP) in phase I vs 0% ethanol. Specific substrate/product rates experimental but graphical.

growth_records.cite: Contreras, Angela et al. Mapping the Physiological Response of Oenococcus oeni to Ethanol Stress Using an Extended Genome-Scale Metabolic Model. Frontiers in Microbiology (2018). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2018.00291. [PMC5838312]
growth_records.snippet: 12 (ethanol % v/v) Maximum biomass 0.447 gDCW L-1, Maximum specific growth rate 0.013 h-1 (Table 1). ... histidine was the most consumed amino acid in the cultures with 12% ethanol, reaching 18 mmol gDCW-1.
growth_records.notes: mu=0.013/h at 12% ethanol (Table 1); td=ln2/0.013=53.3 h. Max biomass 0.447 gDCW/L. Model estimated ~17x higher phase-I NGAM vs 0% ethanol; F0F1-ATPase flux up to 4.8 and phosphoketolase down to 1.3 mmol/gDCW/h (FBA predictions). Histidine cumulative uptake 18 mmol/gDCW (no time basis). Specific substrate/product rates experimental but graphical.
